coredata.dc:title: Binder-free CuS/ZnS/sodium alginate/rGO nanocomposite hydrogel electrodes for enhanced performance supercapacitors
coredata.dc:creator: You, Yue
Qu, Keqi
Shi, Cai
Sun, Zhe
Huang, Zhanhua
Li, Jian
Dong, Mengyao
Guo, Zhanhu
coredata.dc:description: CuS/ZnS/sodium alginate/reduced graphene oxide nanocomposites (CZSrG) were prepared by physical crosslinking followed by one-step reduction and were justified as green binder-free hydrogel high-capacitance electrodes. The physical crosslinking was realized simply through the hydrogen-bond interaction between sodium alginate (SA) and graphene oxide (GO), avoiding the usage of traditional Ca2+ crosslinking agent. The hydrogel structure made of CZSrG possessed the most beneficial effect of avoiding large volume change and increasing cycle stability for supercapacitors. When used as electrode, the specific capacitance of CZSrG was 992 F·g−1 (10 mV·s−1) in a three-electrode system. Furthermore, the fabricated supercapacitors had a specific capacitance of 252.1 F·g−1 (5 mV·s−1), and a power density of 1800 Wh·kg−1 at the energy density of 2.05 Wh·kg−1. Thus, the CZSrG has a favorable electrochemical performance and wide application prospects in supercapacitors.

head.ce:title: Binder-free CuS/ZnS/sodium alginate/rGO nanocomposite hydrogel electrodes for enhanced performance supercapacitors

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: CuS/ZnS/sodium alginate/reduced graphene oxide nanocomposites (CZSrG) were prepared by physical crosslinking followed by one-step reduction and were justified as green binder-free hydrogel high-capacitance electrodes. The physical crosslinking was realized simply through the hydrogen-bond interaction between sodium alginate (SA) and graphene oxide (GO), avoiding the usage of traditional Ca2+ crosslinking agent. The hydrogel structure made of CZSrG possessed the most beneficial effect of avoiding large volume change and increasing cycle stability for supercapacitors. When used as electrode, the specific capacitance of CZSrG was 992 F·g−1 (10 mV·s−1) in a three-electrode system. Furthermore, the fabricated supercapacitors had a specific capacitance of 252.1 F·g−1 (5 mV·s−1), and a power density of 1800 Wh·kg−1 at the energy density of 2.05 Wh·kg−1. Thus, the CZSrG has a favorable electrochemical performance and wide application prospects in supercapacitors.

ce:section.ce:para: To overcome the depletion of traditional fossil energy and global warming, clean renewable energy sources have attracted increasing interest [1–13]. Among various energy storage devices [14,15,17–21], supercapacitors (SCs) have outstanding electrochemical performance of rapid charge/discharge rates, high specific power density and excellent cycle stability [22–24]. Recently, improving specific capacitance of electrode materials has become the most effective way to meet the demands for high capacitance storage applications [25–30]. Metal sulfide as a high-capacitance electrode material exhibits better electrochemical activity and conductivity than corresponding metal oxide, thus, it has been widely applied in SCs [31–33]. With the advantages of abundant reserves, low-cost and high theoretical capacity, copper sulfide (CuS) has been widely applied in lithium-ion batteries [34]. However, CuS has poor charge-transfer interfacial kinetics, which limits the application in high-capacitance electrode.

Electrodes composed of mixed metallic sulfides can overcome the above limitation because these sulfides can affect each other to yield different microstructures, offer richer redox reactions and work synergistically for SCs [35]. For example, Himasree's group reported a specific capacity of 89.77 mA h g−1 (1 A·g−1) for the CuS@MnS/nickel foam (NF) electrode, which was almost 2 times higher than that of CuS/NF (45.73 mA h g−1). This result proved that the MnS could enhance the specific capacity of pure CuS [36]. Yu et al. added zinc sulfide (ZnS) to flower-like Cu5Sn2S7, and testified that ZnS could increase the conductivity, influence the micromorphology and enhance the specific capacity of Cu5Sn2S7 [37]. ZnS as a p-type semiconductor has high conductivity and electrochemical activity owing to its small band gap of 3.7 eV [38]. Despite these advantages, the problems of aggregation, large volume change, low conductivity and cycle stability of CuS/ZnS are still unsolved.

Integrating CuS/ZnS and conductive carbon material to form composites can resolve effectively the defects of low conductivity and aggregation [39–42]. The reduced graphene oxide (rGO) as an advanced layered carbon material possesses excellent conductivity and large surface area which will provide a large number of electrochemical reactive sites [43–45]. Due to its high mechanical strength, rGO has served as a favorable candidate material for various functional hydrogels [46,47]. Recently, hydrogel electrodes have exhibited the most beneficial effect on avoiding large volume change and increasing cycle stability [48]. As a favorable hydrogel matrix, biomass polysaccharide sodium alginate (SA) has abundant hydroxyl and carboxyl groups which will form hydrogen bonding with GO molecule, and lead to form hydrogel structure [49–52]. In similar reports, the cross-linking agent CaCl2 and the ageing process were required for the SA/GO hydrogel preparation. However, we reported novel method for synthesizing SA/GO including mixing and drying. In this method, cross-linking agent was not needed and the process of ageing could be omitted. Thus, our synthetic method was simple, low-cost and rarely reported in previous studies [54,55]. The obtained 3D net structure of SA/rGO hydrogel can accommodate volume changes of CuS/ZnS and promote the transport of OH− and electrons. Meanwhile, organic binder polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) was not required for preparing hydrogel electrodes [56].

In this study, we showed a hydrogel electrode composed of CuS/ZnS/SA/rGO (CZSrG) via physical crosslinking and one-step reduction. Morphological features and chemical composition of CZSrG were analyzed by various characterizations. The results showed that the GO was reduced successfully and the CuS/ZnS was wrapped in SA/rGO hydrogel. For electrochemical performance, compared with other CuS electrodes, the CZSrG exhibited excellent specific capacitance of 992 F·g−1 (10 mV·s−1). The addition of ZnS successfully increased the specific surface area and active sites of CuS. Meanwhile, the obtained CuS/ZnS was wrapped in SrG hydrogel and the volume change was accommodated. These routes were different from traditional CuS electrodes and led to better electrochemical performances and excellent application prospects in supercapacitors.

Copper (II) nitrate (AR), zinc nitrate (AR), sodium alginate (CP), thiourea (AR), potassium hydroxide (AR) and ascorbic acid (AR) were purchased from different companies. Graphene oxide was prepared in our laboratory. The detailed information was shown in Supporting Information.

The CuS/ZnS was prepared by the hydrothermal method. Briefly, 2.5 mmol CuNO3 was dissolved in 72 mL deionized water followed by stirring for 10 min, and then 2.5 mmol of ZnNO3 was added and stirred for 10 min. Finally, 10 mmol thiourea was added and stirred for another 10 min. This uniform solution was transferred to a 100 mL autoclave and heated to 180 °C for 12 h. The obtained sample was washed for three times with ethanol and deionized water, respectively, followed by drying at 60 °C for 12 h. For comparation, CuS and ZnS were also prepared in the absence of ZnNO3 or CuNO3 and half of thiourea under the same experimental conditions [57].

Certain amount of CuS/ZnS was dispersed in 10 mL deionized water followed by ultrasound for 10 min to acquire solution A. Meanwhile, 0.1 g of SA was put into a 50 mL beaker with 10 mL deionized water and stirred for 30 min, then certain amount of GO (prepared with the method of Hummers) aqueous solution (10 g/L, after ultrasonic for 30 min) was poured into this beaker and stirred for 10 min to obtain solution B [58]. Then solution A was mixed with solution B and stirred for another 30 min to obtain uniform viscous slurry. The slurry was poured on a plate glass and a glass rod was used to lay the liquid flat on the plate glass. After that, the plate glass was placed in a drying oven and dried for 12 h at 60 °C to obtain the CZSG. The dried CZSG was putted into 250 mL beaker with 10 g/L ascorbic acid solution (100 mL) for 30 min at 90 °C. After the reduction process, the CZSG was washed with DI water and soaked in it for 4 h, and then dried at 60 °C for 12 h to obtain CZSrG. When used as electrode, this slurry was coated on a 1 cm × 2 cm nickel foam flake instead of plate glass, and other steps were the same as CZSrG preparation. For comparison, CuS/SA/rGO and ZnS/SA/rGO (CSrG and CSrG) electrodes were prepared in the absence of ZnS and CuS under the same experimental conditions. The preparation process of CZSrG is shown in Fig. 1 .
The CZSrG electrodes were characterized by SEM, TEM, FTIR, XPS, XRD and TGA. The characterization details were shown in Supporting Information.

The CZSrG was assembled to three-electrode system, two-electrode system and CZSrG//SrG device to exam the electrochemical properties, and the detailed process and equations were shown in Supporting Information.

The microstructures of the CuS, CuS/ZnS, and CZSrG were studied by SEM and TEM. As shown in Fig. 2d, the CuS/ZnS was composed of CuS which had a flower-like structure and ZnS which was proofed as uniform spheres with pleated surfaces. Fig. 2a shows that the flower-like CuS particles were made up with 2D sheets. As shown in the inset diagram, the 2D sheets presented a regular hexagonal structure and the CuS composed of them could provide abundant active sites for electrochemical contacts [37]. The uniform distribution of Cu and S for CuS was proved by the elemental mapping and EDAX analysis (Fig. S2d, e and g). The corresponding TEM is shown in Fig. 2b, which further proofed the flower-like structure of CuS. As shown in Fig. 2c, the crystal interplanar spacings of 0.282 and 0.298 nm were ascribed to the (103) and (100) planes of CuS. The morphology of ZnS was shown in Fig. S2a and proofed as uniform spheres with pleated surfaces. The corresponding elemental mappings and EDAX analysis (Fig. S2b, c and f) proved the uniform distribution of Zn and S elements.

Fig. 2d showed the microstructures of CuS/ZnS. Compared with CuS, the addition of ZnS made the sheets in each CuS particle more plentiful. This increased the surface sites for electrochemical reaction and improved the electrochemical performance of CuS. The corresponding TEM (Fig. 2e) testified the structure of CuS and ZnS in Fig. 2d. Meanwhile, the crystal interplanar spacing of 0.31 nm was ascribed to the (111) plane of ZnS. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) shown in Fig. 2f was indexed to the (103) and (100) planes of CuS and (200) plane of ZnS, respectively. To prove the 3D net structure of SrG hydrogel, the inner structure of SrG was shown by SEM in Fig. S1, which proved the regular grid structure. Meanwhile, the SEM of CZSrG (Fig. 2g) exhibited that the CuS/ZnS was uniformly wrapped by SrG hydrogel which could be proofed by elemental mappings (Fig. 2h–k) and EDAX analysis (Fig. 2l). As shown in Fig. 2m–q, the flexibility of CZSrG was tested by bending and twisting, and when the CZSrG was released, it could return to the original shape.

Fig. 3a shows the FT-IR spectra of the samples. For the GO, the absorption peaks at 1733, 1042, 1620 and 1409 cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibrations of CO, C-O-C, CC and C-OH, respectively. The broad peak band at 3200– 3400 cm−1 referred to the stretching vibrations of -OH. For the SA, the absorption peaks at 1595, 1418 and 1026 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of the CC, C-OH and C-O-C, respectively [59,60]. For the CZSG, the adsorption peaks of -OH, CC, C-OH and C-O-C at 3289, 1601, 1418 and 1025 cm−1 are almost consistent with those in GO and SA, indicating that the CuS/ZnS was successfully wrapped in the SA/GO gel matrix. For the CZSrG, the characteristic peaks of oxygenic groups as C-OH and C-O-C were significantly weakened, indicating that the GO was successfully reduced.

The elemental composition and valence state of CuS/ZnS were analyzed by XPS. Fig. 3b, c and d showed the full-, Cu-, Zn-and S-spectra of CuS/ZnS, respectively. The peaks of Zn 2p appeared at 1021.3 and 1044.4 eV and the split orbit was calculated as 23.1 eV, assigning to Zn (II). The spectrum of Cu 2p showed two peaks at 931.8 and 951.6 eV with a splitting of 19.8 eV. However, this result could not be used to tell the difference between CuS and Cu2S owning to their close values. However, the valence state of Cu could be distinguished by the binding energy of S 2p. The S 2p peaks of CuS/ZnS were located at 161.2 and 162.3 eV, which were consisted of spin-orbit coupling S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2, respectively, proving the existence of S2− in CuS/ZnS [61]. These results were consistent with other literatures [62,63]. The full spectra of CZSrG, Fig. 3e, reveal the existence of the Zn, Cu, O, C and S in the composite hydrogel.

To further investigate the reduction of GO in CZSG. Fig. 3f and g shows the C-spectra and O-spectra of CZSG and CZSrG, respectively. In C-spectra, for the CZSG, the chemical binding energies at 288.1, 287.0, 286.0 and 284.6 eV belong to O-C=O, CO, CO and CC. Thus, the CO, CO and CC still existed, which was consistent with FT-IR results. For the CZSrG, O-C=O, CO, CO and CC almost appeared at the same chemical binding energy as 288.1, 286.7, 285.8 and 284.2 eV. However, comparing these functional groups in CZSrG and CZSG, the peak strength of CO was significantly weakened, meanwhile, the peak strength of CC was markedly enhanced. In the O-spectra, comparing the strength of OH (531.5 eV) in CZSrG and CZSG, it could be seen that the OH peak strength was reduced. Combining these results with FT-IR, it could be seen that through one-step reduction, the GO in CZSG was successfully reduced.

XRD patterns of the GO, SA/GO, CuS/ZnS and CZSrG are shown in Fig. 3h. The sharp diffraction peaks at 2θ = 10.8° and 42.8° correspond to the faces (001) and (101) of graphitic plane (PDF 03-065-6212) in GO. The sharp diffraction peaks at 2θ = 27.6°, 47.8°and 56.4° correspond to the (111), (220) and (311) faces of ZnS (JCPDS 83-1990) [64]. Meanwhile, the diffraction peaks at 2θ = 28.6°, 31.7°, 32.7°, 52.6° and 59.2° correspond to the (102), (103), (006), (108) and (116) faces of CuS (JCPDS 06-0464) [65]. Comparing the curves of CuS/ZnS and CZSrG, the diffraction peaks of ZnS and CuS were reserved and barely disappeared, indicating that CuS/ZnS was successfully composited with SA/rGO matrix. However, the peak strength was weakened and it was because that their peaks were superimposed with SA which had no diffraction peak, leading to the reduction in the peak strength. Compared with CZSrG curve, the peak (2θ = 10.8°) of SA/GO curve disappeared, and the characteristic peak (2θ = 27.0°) of rGO appeared, indicating that the GO was successfully reduced to rGO. This result was coincident with FT-IR and XPS characterizations. The TGA and DTG curves are shown and analyzed in Fig. S3 of Supporting information, indicating that the one-step reduction was efficient for GO reduction, and this result agreed well with the above characterizations.

The electrochemical properties such as CV, GCD and EIS were tested through a three-electrode system in 2 M KOH aqueous electrolyte. For CZSrG, the CV curves tested at the scan rates of 10, 30, 50, 80 and 100 mV·s−1 are shown in Fig. 3a. According to the CV curves, a pair of redox peaks appeared in every curve rather than ideal rectangular of electric double layer capacitance (EDLC), which proved the pseudo-capacitive characteristic of CZSrG. With increasing the scan rate, the area of CV increased gradually, meanwhile the anodic peaks moved to the higher potential while the potential of cathodic cathode peaks decreased to a lower value. It was because a larger scan rate could provide a faster charge-discharge rate and satisfy the electronic neutralization. According to the integrated area of CV curves, the specific capacitance of SrG with different masses of GO (the mass ratio of SA and GO was 1:2, 1:1, 2:1 and 3:1) was calculated as 47, 73, 30 and 33 F·g−1 at the scan rate of 10 mV·s−1 as shown in Fig. S4. It proved that the GO could increase the capacitance of SA hydrogel due to the redox reaction of GO, but when the mass of GO was larger than SA, the agglomeration would happen and led to the reducing of capacitance. The typical CV curves of SrG and CSrG electrode were also measured and shown in Fig. S5a. The specific capacitances of SrG, CSrG and CZSrG were calculated to be 74 F·g−1, 552 F·g−1 and 993 F·g−1 at the scan rate of 10 mV·s−1, as shown in Fig. 3b, indicating that CuS had excellent electrochemical performance and ZnS enhanced its property. To study the influence of mass ratio on electrochemical property, a series of electrodes as CZSrG-1, CZSrG-3, CZSrG-5 and CZSrG-7 were prepared and their CV curves were shown as Fig. S5b. As shown in Fig. 3b, the CZSrG-3 had the highest specific capacitance among these four samples.

The electrochemical performances were further examined by GCD measurement. The GCD curves of CZSrG are shown in Fig. 4c. According to the GCD curves, the calculated specific capacitance decreased with increasing the current density. It was because the increasing of current density led to a larger resistance and inadequate Faradaic redox reaction [66]. As shown in GCD curves, the discharge and charge curves were almost symmetrical, indicating the rapid I-V response and good reversibility. From the GCD curves of SrG, CSrG (Fig. S5c, d) and CZSrG, the calculated specific capacitances are shown in Fig. 4d. For CZSrG, the specific capacitances were 654, 637, 629, 584 and 558 F·g−1 at the current density of 1, 3, 5, 8 and 10 A·g−1, respectively, which were higher than SrG and CSrG. This result was consistent with corresponding CV curves.

Further, cycling stability was a significant factor for supercapacitor in practical applications. The capacitance retention of the CZSrG electrode is also shown in Fig. 4e. It could be seen that for CZSrG electrode, after 1000 charge-discharge cycles, there was 70% of the initial specific capacitance retained at a high current density of 10 A/g, indicating an excellent cycling stability. The inset of Fig. 4e shows the simulated diagram of three-electrode system.

EIS analysis was carried on to research the transfer kinetics of fundamental electron. The Nyquist plots of CZS and CZSrG electrode were recorded in the frequency of 100,000– 0.01 Hz as shown in Fig. 4f. The Nyquist plot mainly consisted of a negligible semicircle and an inclined line, at the high-middle and low frequency regions, respectively. For the semicircle, the diameter was related to the charge transfer resistance (Rct) caused by Faradic reactions. Meanwhile, the inclined line referred to the process of mass transfer, and the slope referred to ions diffusive resistance and known as Warburg impedance (Zw). The intercept was associated with solution resistance (Rs), which was a composite resistance including the ionic, inherent and contact resistance of aqueous solution, foam nickel and electrolyte/electrode, respectively. The corresponding circuit diagram was also shown in the inset of Fig. 4f. For the high-middle frequency region, the Rct value of CZS, CSrG, CZSrG-1, CZSrG-3, CZSrG-5 and CZSrG-7 was determined as 0.07254, 0.78137, 1.673 × 10−5, 1.571 × 10−6, 3.203 × 10−5 and 0.14192 Ω, respectively, as shown in Table 1 . This indicated that the ZnS could increase the conductivity of CuS, further the addition of rGO observably increased the conductivity of CuS/ZnS and led to a lower intrinsic resistance. Meanwhile, the CZSrG-3 had the lowest intrinsic resistance and the best charge transport behavior among CZSrG-1, CZSrG-3, CZSrG-5 and CZSrG-7. The Rs value of the six electrodes was 1.19, 0.7833, 0.7927, 0.6518, 0.7189 and 0.6809 Ω, respectively, indicating the effect of ZnS and rGO on increasing the conductivity and reducing solution resistance, meanwhile CZSrG-3 had the lowest solution resistance. At the low frequency region, the slope of CZSrG-3 was larger than others, indicating a low Zw and fast electron ion diffusion rate at the interface of electrode/electrolyte. According to these results, the CZSrG-3 was prepared in large quantity and used for the following experiments.

The values of specific capacitance were also compared with CuS electrodes reported in other literatures, as shown in Table S1. The specific capacitance of CZSrG electrode was 1.7 times larger than that of CuS-CTAB electrode at the current density of 1 A·g−1 [67]. Compared with CuS-OEs/rGO electrode, although at a higher scan rate of 10 mV/s, the specific capacitance of CZSrG electrode was 4.6 times larger than that of CuS-OEs/rGO electrode at the low scan rate of 5 mV·s−1 [68]. Thus, CZSrG electrode exhibited outstanding electrochemical properties compared with other CuS electrodes.

The outstanding electrochemical capacitive of CZSrG could be attributed to its unique hydrogel structure and excellent active electrode material CuS/ZnS. On one hand, as shown in Fig. 5 , the SA/GO hydrogel was formed by hydrogen bonds which ensured the structural stability during reducing and performances testing. Then, the CuS and ZnS were uniformly anchored on the surface and inside of SA/GO hydrogel matrix, avoiding the aggregation of CuS and ZnS particles. Meanwhile, the 3D hydrogel structure could provide abundant electrochemical active sites, increasing the contact area between the electrolyte and electrode. Moreover, the rGO acted as an excellent conductive agent, ensured electron transfer and electrolyte ion diffusion in the process of charge/discharge. On the other hand, CuS/ZnS had excellent electrochemical performance. As shown in Fig. 4a, the CZSrG had a pair of remarkable reversible redox peaks, and the anodic peak corresponded to the conversion of CuS and CuSOH, while the cathodic peak was the reversible process. The Faradic reaction of CZSrG electrode in KOH electrolyte was described by Eq. (1) [69,70].
(1)
CuS +
OH −
↔ CuSOH +
e −

Moreover, in the energy storage process, the CuS/ZnS provided two mechanisms as faradic and EDLC [71]. To testify that more than one mechanism existed in CuS/ZnS, the electrochemical kinetics was studied in details and shown in Supporting Information. As shown in Eq. (S5) of Supporting Information, the b value of 0.5 indicated a faradic intercalation process while the b value of 1.0 was on behalf of a capacitive behavior. According to the logi and logv, the fitting curves are shown in Fig. S6a, the b value was equal to 0.63 and 0.56, which revealed that the faradic intercalation and capacitive behavior were all contributory in the charge storage process, and pseudo-capacitive contribution was increased gradually from 88.14% to 95.92% with the growth of scan rate (Fig. S6b).

The practical utilization of CZSrG was further explored in a symmetric supercapacitor device. The device was fabricated based on two CZSrGs as positive and negative electrodes in 2 M KOH. The CV curves of above symmetric supercapacitor were measured at the scan rates of 5– 100 mV·s−1. As shown in Fig. 6a, the CV curves were similar to rectangles and the redox peaks were almost nonexistent, which were different from the three-electrode system, indicating that this supercapacitor was charged and discharged at pseudo constant rates [72]. At the range of 5– 100 mV·s−1, the shapes of CV curves were unvaried, demonstrating high I-V responses and electron transport rates of the symmetric supercapacitor. According to the area of CV curves, the capacitance was calculated to be 252.1 F·g−1 at the scan rate of 5 mV·s−1.

Meanwhile, the GCD curves of CZSrG were tested at the current densities of 1– 3 A·g−1, as shown in Fig. 6b. The capacitance was calculated to be 52.3 F·g−1 at the current density of 1 A·g−1. The most important parameter of the supercapacitor application was energy density (E) and power density (P). When E decreased from 10.45 to 2.05 Wh·kg−1, P increased from 600 to 1800 Wh·kg−1. Furthermore, the corresponding Nyquist plot of EIS spectrum for CZSrG in the supercapacitor was recorded in the frequency of 100,000– 0.01 Hz as shown in Fig. 6e. The Rs and Rct were calculated to be 0.7029 and 0.1159 Ω, respectively, indicating excellent electrical conductivity of CZSrG in supercapacitor. The corresponding circuit diagram was also shown in the inset of Fig. 6e.

Meanwhile an asymmetric supercapacitor device was built with CZSrG positive electrode and SrG negative electrode in 2 M KOH. Fig. 6c and d shows the CV and GCD curves. The corresponding capacitances were calculated to be 143.5 F·g−1 (5 mV·s−1) and 71.4 F·g−1 (0.5 A·g−1), respectively. As for E and P, when E decreased from 6.85 to 3.19 Wh·kg−1, P increased from 157.1 to 627.5 Wh·kg−1 at the current densities of 0.5– 3 A·g−1. Finally, according to Nyquist plot (Fig. 6f), Rs and R1 were calculated to be 0.75401 and 0.003306 Ω, respectively. The circuit diagram was also shown in the inset of Fig. 6f.

To further investigate the operating principle of CZSrG in supercapacitor, in Fig. 7a, the 3D schematic model of energy storage mechanism for this symmetric and asymmetric supercapacitor was illustrated. As shown in the 3D schematic model, the possible ions and charges transfer processes were simulated as K+ and OH− ions moving towards the negative and positive electrodes, respectively, during the charge/discharge process. The supercapacitor worked by redox reactions with the adsorption/desorption OH− on positive electrode and simultaneous adsorption/desorption of K+ on negative electrode [73]. Fig. 7b shows the 3D model diagram and photograph of a light emitting diode (3 V) powered by the CZSrG//SrG devices.

In this study, a green binder-free hydrogel high-capacitance electrode CZSrG was designed and used for building supercapacitors. The method, combining the physical crosslinking of SA and GO through the hydrogen-bond interaction and one-step reduction, was simpler, safer, more efficient and low-cost. The specific capacitance of CZSrG electrode was calculated to be 992 F·g−1 (10 mV·s−1) in the three-electrode system. When fabricated as supercapacitors, the specific capacitance was calculated to be 252.1 F·g−1 (5 mV·s−1). On account of excellent electrochemical performance and eco-friendly features, novel hydrogel electrode CZSrG had wide application prospects in supercapacitors.
Yue You, Keqi Qu, Cai Shi and Zhe Sun: Data curation; Methodology; Investigation; Roles/Writing - original draft.

Mengyao Dong: Validation; Data curation.
